author: @jasonmorello1374
text: I am working(as I had access to a bunch of free sample material) trying a similar process with Anthracite coal, as from the look of it, it should be a dirty 90% graphite from the look of it. I am having some luck, but since I started it recently and first watched this ages ago, I neglected to remember a couple points in the, which may have made my first run a very mixed bag. I am using a steel electrode, and forgot to swap voltages back and forth as a regular occurence. I got a lot of activity eventually in one direction, coal on the negative pole, but also pulled a lot of iron (3) sulfate into the bath. I am going to reverse poles for a bit and see if that helps, but also the parts pulling off are at least denser as they are not floating. Just  reverse poles again now, and already the solution is showing the green of iron(2) sulfate becoming populated. It may pull all the Fe back out in a while similar to my run, but I am betting I may need a cleaning process for non carbon content anyway.

author: @faceless_acts9974
text:
Dear Mr. Smith,

Hi! I am very interested in your videos; it seems like a lot of your interests coincide with mine, and it's actually a little scary! 
Anyways, I have two questions posed to you and was hoping you could help answer them. I am just entering high school and I've begun to study chemistry on my own, but my knowledge and intuition are definitely way below acceptable standards, so I need some help.

The first question is: how can one utilize CO2 or some other greenhouse gas to produce graphene? I personally believe this is the truest form of "green graphene synthesis" where we take our worst fears and turn it into something we'd all love to have. Do you have any thoughts for a procedure to do this? 

The second question has a little to do with the first. That is: how do you know what chemicals or solutions to use in a procedure? You mentioned in the video that one must have an ionic solution (100 mL H20 and 2.4g of sulfuric acid at 98%), but why? You mentioned in an earlier video that you want to break the bonds of the graphite in the "c axis" (I think that's what you said, but I'm probably wrong) when dissolving it in a solution, but then this leads to my next question of: how do you know which solvent is better than another and what should the proper concentration be? I feel like the ideal solvent contains the optimum polarity of molecules able to break the bonds of the graphene and then the optimum concentration of the molecules, but is this the case? 
And then for the 30% sodium hydroxide solution, how does one know the proper concentration of this solvent and that this chemical solution will achieve the desired effect of reducing the graphene's thickness? Can one only find out through rigorous testing of different potential solutions and their concentrations, or are they supposed to know the properties of this solution and just know it will work? I'm interested in hearing your answer to this question and maybe we can figure out how it may be applied to building a procedure for the first question above. 

Thank you so much for your help! Even if you can't answer my questions, I greatly appreciate your work in posting all of these videos for free. You are such a great and kind man!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Hiya mate - I am afraid there are no easy answers to give. The CO2 one is easy enough burn some magnesium in some dry ice and hey presto. there are quite a few papers on this and a few youtube vids too. For the rest of your questions that's just a matter of learning and time

replies.author: @faceless_acts9974
replies.text: Thanks for simply bothering to read everything that I wrote above. It means a lot to me! Anyways, are you saying that with more testing and experimentation, one will learn what chemicals and appropriate concentrations to use, or acquiring a broader understanding of chemistry will do the trick? Or is it a combination of both? I'm curious to know what steps I should take in moving forward, so do you have any advice? Thanks!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's a combination of both mate and the best advice is read and experiment

author: @johncorer868
text: Hi. Thanks for the video. Other than sulfate ions, what other ions are capable of exfoliating graphite into layers? I had heard of some people using NaS...I am aware that only certain ions have the correct shape and size to be able to do this...Is there a way I find out which ions are able to exfoliate graphite into graphene? Thanks.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +John Corer a ton of things will intercalate - sometimes the methodology isn't obvious - like using a smaller ion as an intercalation assistant to larger ions - CuCl2 helps the intercaltion of Al for example - your best bet is to do a google scholar search

replies.author: @johncorer868
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith  So, is there a specific reason why pure NaCl won't intercalate graphite? Is this purely because the cation and anion are too large, or are there more components to it

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +John Corer there are other components - think of it like splitting a log

Robert Murray-Smith 
Hehe, okok, I have actually heard/seen you say this a few times ;) You have tried so many different methodologies and shared how to do that on your channel, so you have a rich experience with how the different forms of graphene and graphite behaves. And I figure you want us to experiment on our own and discover by ourselves what is more appropriate for our end products.
I guess I can do that, and I am grateful for you giving us the tools to do so.
And I can imagine you have almost more or less shared everything that is needed to replicate your EESD, directly and indirectly, over the years on your channel. So I just have to get going then :)
thanks Robert.

author: @sveindanielsolvenus
text:
Hi

Thank you for your work here. I was so happy to discover your supercapacitor work, I really love that you try making it as homemade as possible and showing it to us. A life saver.

One thing I am wondering about is the filters. I am a little confused. In your book you say 100nm, which equals 0.1microns. Which is a little difficult to come by, but not impossible. Did you actually mean 100microns? Which is 100000nm. One of your comments here, from two years ago, you wrote micron 10-100.
Here are 100 micron filters:http://www.ebay.co.uk/sch/i.html?_from=R40&_trksid=p2050601.m570.l1313.TR2.TRC1.A0.H0.Xsulfuric+acid.TRS0&_nkw=100micron+filter&_sacat=0
Here are almost 0.1micron filters: http://www.ebay.co.uk/sch/i.html?_odkw=mebrane+filters&_osacat=0&_from=R40&_trksid=p2045573.m570.l1313.TR0.TRC0.H0.Xmembrane+filter.TRS0&_nkw=membrane+filter&_sacat=0

May I ask another question? How is potassium hydroxide much better than sodium hydroxide? And would I use 11ml of 30% potassium hydroxide also? Or just the right amount to get the electrolyte to pH 1.2?

By the way. I found wonderful graphite electrodes for cheap on ebay: http://www.ebay.co.uk/sch/i.html?_odkw=graphite+electrode&_osacat=0&_from=R40&_trksid=p2045573.m570.l1313.TR0.TRC0.H0.TRS0&_nkw=graphite+electrode&_sacat=0

I like ebay.

I want to make similar EESD as you are showing on your channel. That is why I want to make graphene. I hope this method will help.

For the capacitor itself, I currently think I will mix the graphene with PVA and water, paint that on aluminumfoil, use glass fiber as separator, and an electrolyte of maybe 0.6g urea mixed with 4.82g aluminum chloride hexahydrate or PVA mixed with phosphoric acid.
I really hope to get to very high energy density. 
I am trying my best to scrape together the knowledge you are sharing in your many videos. I just discovered you a day ago.

Cheers!

Btw. 
I think the 555 timer method for reversing polarity needs two 555s, set in a H-bridge.
I found this, as an alternative with arduino: http://www.instructables.com/id/Super-Easy-Reversible-Motor-Control-for-Arduino-/?ALLSTEPS

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +sveindaniel it depends what you want to do. If you want the graphene to pass through then you must use a filter paper bigger than the graphene but smaller than the graphite. If you want to stop the graphene from passing through - for example to make graphene paper - you need it smaller than the graphene

replies.author: @sveindanielsolvenus
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith  
Yes, of course. Ok, and 0.1 micron is needed to stop it from passing through. Ok.
Thanks.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +sveindaniel no worries mate

author: @chaitanyabhave2092
text:
I read a paper in which they used sodium hydroxide,sodium thiosulphate and sodium hypochlorite to do this process. The basic medium prevents oxidation of the graphene sheets, and I believe it works with a single polarity(graphite as anode). I did this, and got the same kind of black floating film. Unfortunately it foams like mad. Here's the link: http://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlelanding/2015/ra/c5ra06404j#!divAbstract
It turns out that you can use sulphate salts in alkaline solutions(Na2SO4+NaOH?) instead of the acid, so even greener.
Oh, and the graphene solution went bluish, like you predicted. Thanks for the constant feedback and guidance.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sorry mate - i missed this comment a new concept called the electrochemical flow capacitor (EFC) has been presented in several papers. This new concept shares the major advantages of both supercapacitors and flow batteries, providing rapid charging/discharging while enabling the decoupling of the power and energy ratings. Like in supercapacitors, energy is stored in the electric double layer of charged carbon particles. A flowable carbon-electrolyte mixture is employed as the active material for capacitive energy storage, and is handled in a similar fashion to flow or semi-solid batteries (i.e., for charging/discharging, it is pumped into an electrochemical cell, and for storage, it is pumped into reservoirs). Most studies present the proof-of-concept of this technology and report on initial EFC performance data obtained under static and intermittent flow operations. try a googel scholar search

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: replied tot he earlier comment - sorry i missed it

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: see my reply - there is some confusion these days over what constitute a capacitor and what constitutes a battery - a good way of seeing it is as an energy storage device with capacitive attributes

text:
Блин, а купить нормальный блок питания религия не позволяет?
стоит 50 баксов, зато шаг по напряжению 0.1 в, и регулировка тока. Всё это выведено на табло.
По английски ни чего не понимаю, да не суть, если смешивать серную кислоту и гидрооксид натрия,  получаем натрий сернокислый, так как кислота в недостатке, то имеем гидрооксид и соль натрия. Если щелочь в недостатке имеем соль и кислоту.
Если я не ошибаюсь, то реакция внедрения идет только при кислоте, а в щелочи идет просто разрушение графитового электрода, но графеном там и не пахнет.Слишком большие гранулы. Пытался отмыть и в микроволновке вспенить, толку ноль(

Пробовал, не получилось.
Может не правильно перевел.

Если только использовать серную кислоту и нитрат натрия, но опять же начнет выделятся азотная кислота, она то и внедрится, а за ней серная часть состава.
А вот если внедрить калий азотнокислый, получим порох)))))
Остается высушить, и заряжай патроны. Сажа( графит) с селитрой)

replies.author: @ПавелБаранов-д5ц
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith ﻿
Я делал на азотной кислоте 90% и серной кислоте,  там есть одна большая проблема нужно много воды для отмывки термо расширеного графита,  пробовал электролизом, анодное внедрение, но опять же большое колличество воды в расходе, на следующей неделе задумал проверить возможность расслоенные графита методом Тау-Лао с сахаром, думаю мед  и натуральный тросниковая патока будут лучшими вариантами.
Очищение графена от сахара проведу реакцией брожения и получением спирта спирт выпарю, дрожжи убью щелочью, короче нужен вариант с малымы промывными водами.
А то получается выход материала мал, а воды идет ведрами(
Спасибо.
С приветом из России. Дальний восток.

PS. Your actually my first real chemistry teacher as a matter of fact.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: The easiest thing to do is build one. Radio shack or maplin have a kit available with relay included or you can just design your own based on a 555 chip. There's lots of info on the net on how to go about doing this and you need a 555 chip a couple of capacitors and a couple of resistors to do it

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: To 100% prove you have graphene your going to need to measure the thickness of the plates you have formed. To do that you are going to need access to some analytical tools like an electro microscope. If you don't have those kind of things available you will have to spin cast your solution anneal it and if you get a transparent coating that conducts to some degree you will have a confidence levels of graphene.

Good day sir, 
I have contacted you a few months ago, i had questions about my project. I don't know if you can remember, it was booked in the summer. author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Graphene doesn't actually dissolve in water it disperses to form a colloidal solution and not much disperses at that. To improve the amount dispersed you need to add a surfactant PVP will do a good job as will PEG. This means you can just paint it on. however you have to get rid of the surfactant afterwards and this is done by heating so the substrate needs to be able to withstand temperatures up to 300 degrees centigrade for half an hour. Glass would be ideal.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: supercapacitors are really easy to make. they are two layers of metal with some conductive material spread on them, a separator is put between them this can be paper and finally an electrolyte which can be salt water, acid or a whole host of stuff. To experiment you can change any of these components. if you want to use grapheme put that on the metal and off you go. But, to be honest, read more before you start as this little description is something you really should understand.

author: @alaasadek4125
text:
And here is another math part.
Now for the KOH 30% part. Assuming that the solid flakes of the KOH are 85% pure and we need 30% in the 100ml solution of mentioned above (2.4grams of sulfuric acid in 100ml of distilled water); then we need to add 30*(100/85)=35.29 grams of solid KOH to that solution.  Is that right?

author: @alaasadek4125
text:
Dear Robert can you check my mathes please?
If the specific gravity of the sulfuric acid is 1.2865, this means that in every cm3 we have 1.2865 grams of solid sulfuric acid. Thus if we want 2.4 grams of sulfuric acid we should use 2.4/1.2865=1.891 cm^3. Since 1ml = cm^3 then we must add 100-1.891=98.108 cm^3 of distilled water. This way we will have 2.4 grams of sulfuric acid in 100ml of distilled water. Is that right?

author: @drmn4ea
text: Sorry, did not see your newest video yet where you cover this exact method. The use of hardware store piping for an autoclave is brilliant!

author: @drmn4ea
text: I think this is a different one - the title is "Bottom-up synthesis of large-scale graphene oxide nanosheets", by Libin Tang, Xueming Li and half a dozen other authors. No graphite or electrolysis is used in this process. I'm not a chemist, but the gist of it seems to be cooking sugarwater (glucose, fructose or sucrose, ~ 0.5M) in an autoclave at ~160-180C until a 'skin' of GO forms on top. A substrate can then be coated by dipping and catching the skin with it.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @nihonbunka
text: Ah, sorry, I see this is an "exfoliation" method that does not form graphene, so much as break it off. So the 2 seconds is to push ions into the graphite and the 5 seconds to pull them and a layer of graphene off (or vice versa). I thought that graphene was being formed, and the 2 secs for creating free ions and the 5 for putting them together. In the latter case the creation and pulling together might be done smoothly but that is not how it is working.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I think you may be over complicating this. The polarity reversal won't affect particle size in itself but rough handling will. If you up the voltage and therefore increase current the flakes get smaller and thicker it's like the difference between gently prising something apart with a wedge as opposed to taking a grater to it. You want enough force to do the job but not so much as to damage things. Your rotating idea may well work but the implementation seems unnecessarily complex - sorry

author: @nihonbunka
text:
One other thing... the 2 second 5 second reversal of the current surely leads to a limit on the size of the graphene films being produced. 

I wonder a rotating electrolyte might obviate the need for the current polarity reversal and make it possible to create a continuous stream of graphene.

The electric current might be modulated (at least in terms of current if not polarity) to resonate with the rotating electrolyte.

Please would you try it, and cut me for 5% should it work :-)

author: @nihonbunka
text:
In your video the graphene is formed on the surface. 

If the surface were flowing then perhaps anything formed on the surface might be more likely to be spread out. 

If spun the surface would be under tension as well, tending to form a greater surface area than it would if it were not spun, since spun bodies of liquid form a u-shaped parabola, lower at the centre. The tension of the surface might tension, and spread out, the graphene. 

Or perhaps I am just thinking of candy floss :-)

This could be achieved by spinning the electrolyte to produce a  flowing u-shaped surface.

author: @picnet
text: I tested copper foil (from a lithium battery) held inside two coffee filter papers + 10% NaOH (Mr Muscle drain cleaner) and graphite/artist block (Not a brilliant source, the flake graphite is on order). Cycling the power at the +-10V stage at around 2 Amps (current limited psu) showed particles flaking off the block. Would be very interesting in the stages used to clean / further process the resulting solution. ps Brought your book - highly recommended :)

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Well, If you happen to have a raman spectroscope like me you can test it - the results are like a fingerprint and pretty much guarantee it is graphene. If not you can disperse it in a solvent - thin film coat it, anneal it at 350 degrees C and see if it conducts. It won't work with coke or coal as these are amorphous and all you are doing is stripping the already there layers - if the layers aren't there - there is nothing to strip. Sulphuric acid is drain cleaner - bullit is good and (cont)

author: @picnet
text: Would it work with other carbon sources, such as coal or coke ? Or even carbon made using the dehydrated sugar method? And again how do you know the end product is actually graphene and not simply carbon gunk? Any tips where to obtain sulphuric acid and sodium hydroxide or equivalent off the shelf products using those chemicals?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Have a look at the earlier comment regarding pencil lead and ionic solutions - to get graphene you need electrolyte control and the NaOH provides this - Though KOH is better.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: The specific size is to give you a baseline for adjustment. Once you have the resistance of your cell you can adjust to maintain current density. It needs cleaning and dispersing - but gentle sonication will do it after after washing under vacuum filtration.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: H2SO4 decomposes to give SO2 and hydrogen. But here is a point at which experimentation will be the best answer.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: In uncontrolled conditions it's likely to be few layer flake graphene if you are lucky - it's most likely to be many layer flake graphite. I only say this because voltage and electrolyte conditions have a big impact on layer thickness. I have heard that pencil lead and ionic liquids like triethyl sulfonium bis (trifluoromethyl sulfonyl) imide works well

author: @joeglory425
text: Supposedly the intercalation compound is C24+ HSO4-.  So what vaporizes?  HSO4- is an ion...or maybe there is some free hydrogen in the lattice which the HSO4- picks up and  it then vaporizes as H2SO4.  My point is that in forming an intercalation if electrons are in excess then something like Na+ or H3O+ will be attracted to the graphite and when polarity is reversed then HSO4- is attracted.  I think that by alternating they can squeeze in better.

Expandable graphite is manufactured and used as a fire retardant.  It heats up, exfoliates, and smothers the fire.  Continued...

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Bless your heart - DMF is what is used and pretty much guaranteed to work. If it was me I'd experiment with other readily available non polar solvents first to see how well they work - for example cellulose thinner, acetone, xylene, toluene - that kind of thing - i would try conductivity tests as a guide to how well it worked - or resistivity tests - just using a multi meter.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I just told her - she is really pleased to hear that. Thanks for buying the books - the money will go straight into research. I'm not really targeting a final thing. I am more concerned with developing processes that anybody can use so we can all experiment. I think one man can't do this - but together we can and what stops us mostly is the knowledge on how to get hold of the basic materials - this takes quite a while to develop so any final supercapacitor is months down the line for me (cont)

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: It depends - graphene isn't a one size fits all - the production method you use affect the qualities of the final product as we are talking about nanomaterials here. Laser is good because it is water soluable, which makes it easy to manage, and it gives a large surface area - green is good because it's 'Safe'  and requires no further processing. Basically you pay your money and take your choice

author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: lol

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: It's amazing how much it will take - actually, by weight it's 3:1 Chloride to graphite. But what you really need to do is vary the proportions by weight and test the results of what you get. So for instance 3:1 is great for conductivity but 2:1 is great for expansion volume. It just depends what you want to do with it. Thanks for buying the book. The money will  help in my research and I am happy to answer any questions you might have - Rob.
